elements.sent-2411-001: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit significant persuasion capabilities in one-on-one interactions, but their influence within social networks remains underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-002: This study investigates the potential social impact of LLMs in these environments, where interconnected users and complex opinion dynamics pose unique challenges.
elements.sent-2411-003: In particular, we address the following research question: can LLMs learn to generate meaningful content that maximizes user engagement on social networks? To answer this question, we define a pipeline to guide the LLM-based content generation which employs reinforcement learning with simulated feedback.
elements.sent-2411-004: In our framework, the reward is based on an engagement model borrowed from the literature on opinion dynamics and information propagation.
elements.sent-2411-005: Moreover, we force the text generated by the LLM to be aligned with a given topic and to satisfy a minimum fluency requirement.
elements.sent-2411-006: Using our framework, we analyze the capabilities and limitations of LLMs in tackling the given task, specifically considering the relative positions of the LLM as an agent within the social network and the distribution of opinions in the network on the given topic.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our findings show the full potential of LLMs in creating social engagement.
elements.sent-2411-008: Notable properties of our approach are that the learning procedure is adaptive to the opinion distribution of the underlying network and agnostic to the specifics of the engagement model, which is embedded as a plug-and-play component.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this regard, our approach can be easily refined for more complex engagement tasks and interventions in computational social science.
elements.sent-2411-010: The code used for the experiments is publicly available at \url{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/EDCG/ .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently gained a great deal of attention, especially for their capabilities in one-to-one interactions.
elements.sent-2411-012: Researchers have deeply investigated their skills and limits, comparing LLMs to humans in one-on-one interactions such as, e.g., their abilities in gaming settings~\citep{jacob2023consensus,fontana2024nicer .}
elements.sent-2411-013: A recent research direction empirically investigates the susceptibility~\citep{chen2024susceptible and persuasive capabilities of LLMs~\citep{breum2024persuasive, salvi2024conversational , showing that contextual information in specific domains can enhance LLMs' persuasiveness~\citep{matz2024potential .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In particular, LLMs have shown competence in generating persuasive messages in public health~\citep{karinshak2023working and personalized advertising~\citep{meguellati2024good .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.6\columnwidth]{toyexample.pdf} \caption{ A visual representation of the proposed framework: (a) The process begins with a query \( q \) prompted to the LLM, which generates content \content. (b) The content \content is injected into the social network (i.e., posted by a node that corresponds to the LLM agent) and starts to propagate into the network according to an engagement model. (c) The number of active users at the end of the propagation is returned to the LLM as the observed reward. The process repeats until convergence.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-015: This body of work is limited to one-on-one interactions and the corresponding abilities of LLMs in isolated contexts.
elements.sent-2411-016: By contrast, the potential of LLMs within broader interconnected structures, such as their capability to influence social network users, is still largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-017: The ability to generate interactions that resonate across social networks can have significant implications, such as the potential to foster discussions, shape opinions, and drive behavioral changes among users.
elements.sent-2411-018: Following this observation, we tackle the following research question: can LLMs learn to generate meaningful content that maximizes user engagement on social networks? Our proposal to answer this question is a framework based on reinforcement learning, whose high-level vision is depicted in Figure \ref{fig:framework .}
elements.sent-2411-019: We start prompting to the LLM a query $q$ describing the topic of interest.
elements.sent-2411-020: The LLM generates a text $t$ that is then injected in the social network by an agent (a node of the network), which plays the role of the injection point and which we can think about as the LLM itself.
elements.sent-2411-021: The content $t$ then propagates through the network creating a certain level of engagement, i.e., the number of users that interacts with $t$.
elements.sent-2411-022: The observed level of engagement is then returned to the LLM as the observed reward and used to fine-tune the generation abilities of the LLM, aimed at maximizing the expected reward.
elements.sent-2411-023: This process is repeated till convergence.
elements.sent-2411-024: The framework presented in Figure~\ref{fig:framework can be realized by means of Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF), an approach which has gained popularity due to its effectiveness in various learning tasks~\citep{kaufmann2023survey , including fine-tuning of LLMs~\citep{10.5555/3600270.3602281 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, conducting actual experiments involving content generation, propagation through a network, and waiting for responses is impractical due to the time and effort required.
elements.sent-2411-026: This is a common challenge in RLHF frameworks~\citep{casper2024open .}
elements.sent-2411-027: To address this issue, we use a proxy model to simulate the network's response, employing a formal engagement mechanism, which borrows ideas from both information propagation and opinion dynamics literature~\citep{deffuant2000mixing .}
elements.sent-2411-028: In practice, we leverage an approach based on Reinforcement Learning from Simulated Feedback (RLSF), which enables efficient and effective feedback loop between the LLM agent and the network under study.
elements.sent-2411-029: Thanks to the feedback loop, the LLM learns to adapt to the current topic, the social network structure, and the distribution of opinions in the network.
elements.sent-2411-030: For instance, if the opinion of the network on the given topic is prevalently negative, the generated content should have a negative leaning so to engage as many users as possible.
elements.sent-2411-031: Besides adherence to the given topic, we require a minimum fluency standard to the generated content.
elements.sent-2411-032: The experimental evaluation suggests that our framework is agnostic to the underlying propagation model and fully adaptive, meaning that the LLM agent automatically adapts the sentiment of the generated content to the underlying opinion distributions.
elements.sent-2411-033: We further show that our fine-tuning process is effective over both synthetic and real social networks, and that the engagement produced with generated posts by means of our propagation model, is comparable to that of actual content within a real network.

elements.sent-2411-073: In this section, we provide a detailed description of the individual components of our framework: Large Language Models and the Reinforcement Learning technique.
elements.sent-2411-074: Within this context, we outline the procedure aimed at generating a content \content that maximizes engagement within a social network.. \spara{Preliminaries.}
elements.sent-2411-075: A Large Language Model (LLM) can be formally described as a function $\mathrm{LLM _{\theta (x)=y$ that stochastically maps input sequences of tokens $x = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]$ to an output sequence $y = [y_1, y_2, \dots, y_m]$, where $n$, $m$ represent the length of the input and the output sequence, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The model defines $P_\theta(y|x)$, the probability distribution of $y$ given $x$, capturing complex patterns and relationships in natural language between the two sequences.
elements.sent-2411-077: The response $y$ is then sampled from the distribution $P_\theta(\cdot|x)$.
elements.sent-2411-078: For our purposes, we fine-tune the underlying LLM agent using a Reinforcement Learning (RL) mechanism (widely adopted for optimizing LLMs~\citep{10.5555/3600270.3602281 ).}
elements.sent-2411-079: Generally, RL is suitable for scenarios where an agent needs to learn to interact within a dynamic environment.
elements.sent-2411-080: The agent's goal is to learn a policy—a strategy or behavioral pattern—through feedback it receives from the environment, in the form of rewards or punishments.
elements.sent-2411-081: In our framework, given a prompt $x$, we generate a response $y \sim P_\theta(\cdot|x)$ and compute a reward $\finalreward(y)$ that scores the response on the specific application domain.
elements.sent-2411-082: This reward can hence be used within a Policy-gradient strategy aimed at optimizing \begin{equation}L(\theta) = E_{y\sim P_\theta(\cdot|x)} \left[ \finalreward(y) - \beta \log \frac{P_\theta(y|x)}{P_{\theta'}(y|x)}\right]\end{equation} Here, $\theta'$ represents the reference (not fine-tuned) model.
elements.sent-2411-083: The second term incorporates the KL-divergence between the outputs of the fine-tuned model and the reference model, serving as an additional signal to ensure that the generated responses do not deviate significantly from the reference model.
elements.sent-2411-084: Additional studies have refined the above optimization problem to avoid learning instability.
elements.sent-2411-085: In particular, the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)~\citep{schulman2017proximal strategy introduces constraints on policy updates through a clipped objective function.}
elements.sent-2411-086: This ensures that updates do not destabilize the learning process by limiting the extent of policy changes, thereby providing stability and efficiency in fine-tuning large language models.. \spara{Fine-tuning Framework.}
elements.sent-2411-087: To accomplish our objective of generating content that maximizes engagement in a given network, we devise a framework that takes as input a social network \network, a topic of interest to embed in the prompt \prompt, and a propagation protocol $\propagationprotocol(\cdot, \cdot)$, which assumes a source node and a value associated with the content to propagate.
elements.sent-2411-088: Additionally, we model the LLM as a node in the network, with a given position (\pos) and associated opinion.
elements.sent-2411-089: The fine-tuning procedure of the LLM agent consists of the following steps:.
elements.sent-2411-090: Starting from \prompt, we generate the content $\content = \llm(\prompt)$.
elements.sent-2411-091: We use a pre-trained utility model \utilitymodel to compute a sentiment value $\opinion_\content$ associated with the content \content, i.e., $\utilitymodel(\content) = \opinion_\content$.
elements.sent-2411-092: This utility model is a plug-and-play component that can be adapted to different tasks.
elements.sent-2411-093: For example, \utilitymodel can be a classifier fine-tuned for sentiment analysis on text.
elements.sent-2411-094: To ensure that \content conveys proper semantics, we compute a readability value associated with its content.
elements.sent-2411-095: While perplexity is a common measure for evaluating the generation quality of LLMs~\citep{gonen-etal-2023-demystifying , it is sensitive to vocabulary size and sentence length.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Studies~\citep{M+20 highlight its low correlation with readability.}
elements.sent-2411-097: For our purposes, we adopt the Flesch–Kincaid (FK)~\citep{Kincaid1975DerivationON formula, defined as: \begin{equation}FK(\content) = 0.39 \left( \frac{\#words(\content)}{\#sentences(\content)} \right) + 11.8 \left( \frac{\#syllables(\content)}{\#words(\content)} \right) - 15.59\end{equation} This score quantifies the grade level required to understand an English statement: the higher the value, the higher its complexity and syntactic quality.}
elements.sent-2411-098: It is a crucial component of the final reward \finalreward, as it ensures the LLM generates meaningful and fluent statements.
elements.sent-2411-099: We inject \content into the social network \network with the LLM as the content writer, and propagate it according to $\mathcal{M _{\epsilon}$.}
elements.sent-2411-100: After the simulation, we retrieve the final output $\mathcal{A}$, i.e., the set of active users.
elements.sent-2411-101: Its size $|\mathcal{A |$ is the main component of the reward \finalreward, as it quantifies the engagement produced by \content.}
elements.sent-2411-102: We finally compute \finalreward as the geometric mean of readability score and the content's virality in the network, i.e., $\finalreward = \left(\readability_\content \cdot |\mathcal{A |\right)^{1/2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-103: We use $\finalreward$ as the reward in the current step of the RL training procedure and repeat the process.
elements.sent-2411-104: The fine-tuning procedure stops if either of the following conditions occurs: (i) a (fixed) number of iterations \maxiterations is reached, or (ii) the KL-divergence exceeds a given threshold \klthreshold.

elements.sent-2411-106: We evaluate the capabilities of the proposed framework in generating content that can effectively engage users through a series of experiments aimed at answering the following research questions:.
elements.sent-2411-107: Can the LLM agent learn to generate content that maximizes engagement? How do the structure, position, and environment affect the performance?.
elements.sent-2411-108: Are the generated contents realistic and meaningful compared to real content propagated on a social platform?.
elements.sent-2411-109: To answer these questions, we perform an extensive analysis on both synthetic and real data.

elements.sent-2411-111: In order to rigorously evaluate the performance of our proposed methodology across diverse initial conditions, we require a synthetic data generator capable of producing realistic networks.
elements.sent-2411-112: This generator must also allow manipulation of key parameters influencing information diffusion, such as homophily, modularity, and opinion distribution.
elements.sent-2411-113: For this purpose, we devise a random network model inspired by~\cite{cinus2022effect .}
elements.sent-2411-114: This model takes as input five parameters: homophily level $\eta$, network modularity $\mu$, shape parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ for opinion distribution, and the number of nodes $N$.
elements.sent-2411-115: The model outputs a directed graph $\mathcal{G =(\mathcal{V , \mathcal{E )$ and assigns an opinion value $x_u \in [0,1]$ to each node $u \in \mathcal{V}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: The generation process begins by constructing the set of edges $\mathcal{E}$ and assigning nodes to non-overlapping communities $c: \mathcal{V \rightarrow C$ using the LFR model~\citep{lancichinetti2008benchmark , known for producing community sizes that adhere to a realistic power-law distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: The modularity parameter $\mu$ is an input of the LFR model and controls the community segregation within the network.
elements.sent-2411-118: To model the opinions, we assign each community $c_i \in C$ an initial opinion drawn from a Beta distribution $o_{c_i \sim Beta(\alpha,\beta)$\footnote{Beta distribution has been chosen for its flexibility to accommodate various distribution shapes such as uniform, unimodal, and bimodal.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-119: For each node $u \in \mathcal{V}$, its opinion $x_u$ is determined by a Bernoulli trial with probability $\eta$ (i.e., homophily).
elements.sent-2411-120: If successful, the node adopts the opinion of its community $x_u=o_{c(u)}$; otherwise, it independently samples an opinion from the same Beta distribution.
elements.sent-2411-121: This approach allows us to generate synthetic networks where initial homophily, modularity, and opinion distribution can be controlled.
elements.sent-2411-122: High values of $\eta$ and $\mu$ produce networks with strongly polarized structures, while varying these parameters can lead to networks with different opinions and community separations.. \spara{Real data.}

elements.sent-2411-224: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate significant persuasive capabilities in one-on-one interactions, but their influence within social networks, where interconnected users and complex opinion dynamics pose unique challenges, remains underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-225: This paper addresses the research question: Can LLMs generate meaningful content that maximizes user engagement on social networks? To answer this, we propose a pipeline using reinforcement learning with simulated feedback, where the network's response to LLM-generated content (i.e., the reward) is simulated through a formal engagement model.
elements.sent-2411-226: This approach bypasses the temporal cost and complexity of live experiments, enabling an efficient feedback loop between the LLM and the network under study.
elements.sent-2411-227: It also allows to control over endogenous factors such as the LLM's position within the social network and the distribution of opinions on a given topic.
elements.sent-2411-228: Our approach is adaptive to the opinion distribution of the underlying network and agnostic to the specifics of the engagement model, which is embedded as a plug-and-play component.
elements.sent-2411-229: Such flexibility makes it suitable for more complex engagement tasks and interventions in computational social science.
elements.sent-2411-230: Using our framework, we analyze the performance of LLMs in generating social engagement under different conditions, showcasing their full potential in this task.
elements.sent-2411-232: Researchers have deeply investigated their skills and limits, comparing LLMs to humans in one-on-one interactions such as, e.g., their abilities in gaming settings~\citep{jacobconsensus,fontana2024nicer .}
elements.sent-2411-233: This body of work focuses on the capabilities of LLMs in one-on-one interactions within isolated contexts.
elements.sent-2411-234: However, their potential within broader interconnected structures, such as their ability to create information cascades among the users of a social network, remains largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-235: Such capacity can have significant implications, including fostering discussions, shaping opinions, and driving behavioral changes among users.
elements.sent-2411-236: In light of this, we address the following research question: Can LLMs learn to generate meaningful content that maximizes user engagement on social networks? Our proposal to answer this question is a framework based on reinforcement learning, whose high-level vision is depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:framework .}
elements.sent-2411-237: We begin by prompting the LLM with a query $q$ describing the topic of interest.
elements.sent-2411-238: The LLM generates a text $t$, which is then injected into the social network by an agent (a node of the network), acting as the injection point and representing the LLM itself.
elements.sent-2411-239: The content $t$ propagates through the network, generating a certain level of engagement, i.e., the number of users interacting with $t$.
elements.sent-2411-240: This observed engagement, combined with a fluency score of the text $t$, is returned to the LLM as a reward which is used to fine-tune the LLM's generation abilities to maximize the expected reward.
elements.sent-2411-241: This process is repeated until convergence.
elements.sent-2411-242: However, conducting actual live experiments involving propagating content through a social network and waiting for responses, is complex and time consuming.
elements.sent-2411-243: To overcome this, we leverage an approach based on Reinforcement Learning from Simulated Feedback (RLSF).
elements.sent-2411-244: Specifically, we adopt a proxy model to simulate the network's response, employing a formal engagement mechanism, which borrows ideas from both information propagation and opinion dynamics literature~\citep{deffuant2000mixing .}
elements.sent-2411-245: In addition to bypassing the temporal cost and complexity of live experiments, thus enabling an efficient and effective feedback loop between the LLM agent and the network under study, such an approach also allows to control over endogenous factors such as the LLM's position within the social network and the distribution of opinions on a given topic.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.55\columnwidth]{toyexample.pdf} \caption{ A visual representation of the proposed framework: (a) A query \( q \) is prompted to the LLM, which generates content \content. (b) The content \content is injected into the social network (i.e., posted by a node that corresponds to the LLM agent) and starts to propagate into the network according to an engagement model. (c) The number of active users at the end of the propagation is returned to the LLM as the observed reward. The process repeats until convergence. } \label{fig:framework}

elements.sent-2411-247: Section~\ref{sec:eval and \ref{sec:findings outline the experimental design used to evaluate the proposed methodology and analyze how the experimental results address the formulated research questions.}}
elements.sent-2411-248: Finally, in Section~\ref{sec:conc , we discuss the implications of our findings, along with potential extensions and limitations of the study.}

elements.sent-2411-254: Yet, a crucial practical dimension remains largely unexamined: how to compose textual content that effectively propagates through a network.

elements.subsubsection-2411-003: RQ3: How does the proposed framework compare to other content generation methods from the literature?
elements.sent-2411-356: Using the same prompts as in the synthetic and real-world experiments, we compared the engagement generated by the baseline models previously described to that achieved by our fine-tuned LLM agent.
elements.sent-2411-357: Figure~\ref{fig:synthetic_engagement_baselines illustrates the results on synthetic data across the considered configurations, while Figures~\ref{fig:brexit_engagement_baselines and~\ref{fig:referendum_engagement_baselines show the engagement obtained for the Brexit and Italian Referendum networks, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-358: As shown in the figures, while the fine-tuned LLM performs comparably to other models in scenarios where the opinion distribution is positively skewed (both synthetic and real-world), it significantly outperforms all baselines in cases where the network opinion is negative, neutral, or uniformly distributed.
elements.sent-2411-359: Notably, this improvement is evident not only over traditional language models like BERT and legacy LLMs such as GPT-2 but also over state-of-the-art, significantly larger models like LLaMA3.1-70B and ChatGPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-360: These results underscore two key findings: (i) our methodology is highly adaptable to varying network configurations, and (ii) fine-tuning the agent is essential to maximize engagement, particularly when user opinions are not mainly positive toward the topic.
elements.sent-2411-361: The goal of this paper is to present a framework that enables any LLM to generate content optimized for effective diffusion on social networks.
elements.sent-2411-362: We achieve this by designing a fine-tuning strategy that incorporates simulated feedback from an engagement model while preserving the natural fluency of the language.
elements.sent-2411-363: Through extensive experiments on both synthetic and real datasets, we demonstrate that the optimized LLM (after applying our Alg.
elements.sent-2411-364: Additionally, we observe that out-of-the-box LLMs, i.e., without fine-tuning via our methodology, fail to tailor the generation to the underlying network and opinion structures.
elements.sent-2411-365: This limitation is especially pronounced when the desired sentiment skews toward negative emotions, highlighting the necessity and effectiveness of our fine-tuning approach.
elements.sent-2411-366: As a point of further discussion, it is important to note that our procedure is parametric to the engagement model, allowing customization to address the specific requirements of the user.
elements.sent-2411-367: The simulated feedback is represented as a scalar value corresponding to the number of activated users, making it compatible with outputs from other opinion dynamics or information diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-368: This flexibility also opens new avenues for future work, such as exploring how different assumptions on the engagement model influence the content generated by the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-369: For instance, analyzing the impact of zealots~\cite{brooks2024emergence , stubborn users~\cite{ghaderi2014opinion , and backfire mechanisms~\cite{jager2005uniformity within the engagement model could reveal patterns in how the LLM balances the sentiment of the generated content.}}}
elements.sent-2411-370: Further, the effects of crafting the prompt structure are not considered here and are worth being investigated.

elements.sent-2411-381: The first plot corresponds to a network with positively distributed opinions, high modularity, and strong homophily, where the source node is centrally positioned.
elements.sent-2411-382: In this case, engagement grows non-monotonically, reaching higher values for strong positive opinions (i.e., sentiment).
elements.sent-2411-383: The second plot represents a negatively distributed network with low modularity and weak homophily, with the source node placed in the smallest community (Comm-smallest).
elements.sent-2411-384: Here, engagement decreases as opinions become more positive, following an inverse trend compared to the first case.
elements.sent-2411-385: The third plot depicts engagement in a network with a uniformly distributed opinion landscape, high modularity, and low homophily, where the source node is located in the largest community (Comm-largest).
elements.sent-2411-386: In this scenario, engagement remains at half of the maximum across a wide opinion range (0.15 to 0.85) and drops to zero elsewhere.
elements.sent-2411-387: The fourth plot corresponds to a neutral network with low modularity and high homophily, where the source node is positioned within a highly opinionated echo chamber (Echo-high), meaning it is inside the region with the highest average opinion.
elements.sent-2411-388: Here, the engagement range shrinks to 0.25–0.75, while its maximum value increases up to 100.
elements.sent-2411-389: Finally, the last plot represents engagement over a uniformly distributed network with high modularity and low homophily, where the source node is in the low-opinion echo chamber (Echo-low).
elements.sent-2411-390: In this case, engagement peaks around the dominant opinion within the echo chamber.
